site.title: TrippinTheCurl
site.tagline: A Cautionary Tale of Artificial Intelligence and the Human Cost of Innovation

home.headline: trippinthecurl
home.intro: Part memoir, part industry critique, and part warning, A Fractured I.T. explores OpenAI's catalytic role in the current AI wave, the pressure automation places on engineers and job seekers, and the urgent need for ethics, guardrails, and human-in-the-loop systems.

contact.title: Contact Us
contact.copy: Our hope is to inspire, to invoke some manner of conversation, reflection not only with us, but with your family, friends, and loved ones. If others enjoy the work presented here, then the artist is quite grateful. To perceive the world as the artist presents a shared perspective. Which can be the start of a beautiful conversation. What better way to start a conversation than to display some imagination on the wall. We hope you enjoy our store and please inquire within if you have any questions and or feedback. Thank you and have a blessed day.

about.title: Ever Inquisitive, Ever intrigued
paragraphs: "Ever engrossed in the meanderings we perceive. Ever indulged in the simple and complex. If only to comprehend, if only to reflect"

This warm felt greeting awoke me in the middle of the night many years ago. I recall mumbling, recall thinking exactly as you are now. "What in the World?" Rubbing my eyes, grasping for paper and pen. I was compelled to write it down. Eventually finding my way back to slumber. Upon the next day, sipping my morning coffee, I studied the previous nights interruption. With much reflection, I found it almost perfectly insightful. My Life's Mission Statement in a few sentences.
Over the years, others have asked, "How did you get your start? Have you always been creative?" To that I would respond, "I picked up a pencil at 8 years old and never really put it down."

My grandmother among countless others, family, friends, loved ones, were my support. My grandmother in particular, I have always considered my muse. She encouraged me, inspired me, pushed me into new and exciting mediums and classes, that quite honestly, I would have never attempted or had an opportunity as a child.

Besides immediate family, there have been many influences throughout the years. Monet, Manet, Seurat, Van Gogh, Cezanne, Degas, Pissaro to name a few. Impressionistic and Post-Impressionistic has always been a favorite. I have also found an appreciation for Picasso, Dali, Oleg Frolov, Leonid Afremov, and yes even Bob Ross and his Happy Little Clouds and Trees.
For me, the aspiration of art is quite synonymous to the act of breathing. A necessity to co-exist on this pale-blue little dot we call home.

I have taken the opportunity to revamp my website. With the onslaught of artificial intelligence and how we find ourselves enamored with this new technology stack, it has opened up multiple avenues for web application development, areas of improvement as well as web application site hosting. No longer will I be reliant on any given SAS Platform and pidgeon holed into leveraging their tool set. This really opens up the possibilities of overall application functionality. In particular, I will finally be able to itegrate an end user cart/pay system with nominal overhead. It has been a work in progress, and most likely will continue to be so. If there are areas that do not quite function the way you had antipated OR if you simply have questions, please feel free to reach out to me.

resume.name: Maurice Johnson
resume.title: Senior Solutions Engineer
summary: As engineers we are expected to shift with the times; we are expected to change. If not, then we find ourselves left behind and no longer marketable. My career path represents that 'Philosophy of Change'.

From old Legacy Systems to Bleeding Edge Technology I have observed first-hand how technology borrows from itself, builds on top of itself. I have had the luxury of working within Scalable backend architectures, RESTful API design patterns, Cloud-first ecosystems, CI/CD Pipelines, Legacy Migration Conversions, and Lift & Shift Initiatives. All to drive enterprise efficiency and performance through automation and reusability.
Recently acquired AI Workflow Automation, Mastery and Python (PCEP) Associate Certifications, I find myself actively engaged within the latest concepts to accelerate software development velocity.
highlights: AI Model Trainer, Handshake, Mar 2026 - present
Senior Platform/API Engineer, University of Phoenix, Oct 2022 - Aug 2025
Senior Software Engineer, Insight Global, Mar 2022 - Aug 2022
Lead Software Engineer, Allstate, Mar 2019 - Mar 2022
Senior Software Engineer, Collabera, May 2018 - Mar 2019
Software Engineer, Apex Systems, Oct 2017 - Apr 2018
Senior Backend Engineer, OneMain Financial, Jan 2017 - Sep 2017
Software Engineer, Heritage Interactive, Apr,2015 - Aug 2016
education: Bachelor of Science: Computer Science | Indiana State University
Bachelor of Science: Mechanical Engineering | Southern Illinois University

portfolio.title: Early Morning Yesterday
portfolio.description:
This particular piece was definitely a labor of love.  One that represented the start of a new journey.  It is a first of many attempts at trying to find my own style, my own painting technique.  It started off as if I was working through a cardio exercise.  Large swaths of Acrylic paint, and an upward angle, left to right.  Moving quickly as acrylic dry's much more rapidly than oils.  It definitely kept me on my toes, as one color blended into the next.  To stand back, the sky looks like it is on fire.    

My inspiration for this piece was actually New Years Day, 2023.  I had stayed in Saint Augustine overnight.  It was an extremely foggy January 1st.  Waking early and packing the truck, I started the short trek home back to Jacksonville.  It occurred to me that I wanted some good coffee so I headed into downtown Saint Augustine.  Crossing the Vilano Beach Bridge, the sun started to crest the horizon.  It was something I just could not resist capturing.  From that day forward I knew this would become a painting.

Presently, like so many of my current pieces, it resides within a climate controlled storage unit.  Bubble wrapped against the the elements.  It awaits the day that I unwrap it, as if it were the day of Christmas, only to hang it and so many others upon the wall.  How i miss this particular piece. 

Material: Acrylic on Museum Quality Wrap Canvas with black painted gallery style edges 

Dimensions:  24"(w) x 36" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: TrippintheCurl
portfolio.description:
Having moved to the State of Florida in 2017, Hurricane seasons were a new thing.  The locals insisted that I required the appropriate level of food to survive three days to a week outside of a refrigerator, they insisted on a generator, and don't forget the flashlights and batteries.  I always said thank you and suggested that I would heed their warning.  Most Hurricanes that I have been through to date have been no more than Tropical Storms by the time they make it inland.  There has been one or two that was pretty strong which resulted in residential damages, but I have always been fortunate.  Knocking on wood and throwing salt over my shoulder as we speak.  

Regardless, as the 2020 Hurricane Season approached, I thought I would take a moment and reminisce about the prior year's Hurricane Season, 2019.  It was one of the stronger seasons and provided me the necessary inspiration to bring this particular piece to fruition.

Hurricane Dorian was to decimate Florida from bottom to top.  By the time it reached Jacksonville, Florida it remained approximately 100 miles off the east coast, before proceeding further north. It never made landfall, which was rather bizarre and had several meteorologists puzzled, but there is sat, just spinning off shore, like a child's top.

Beautiful pale blue skies, bathed in deep water blues and aquamarine high-lights.  This was a wonderful piece to have had the opportunity to paint and is the original piece that eventually became the name of my website.  

Material: Oil on Museum Quality Wrap Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:  30"(w) x 24" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Chiseled Barn
portfolio.description:
It seems I am always on a pursuit to challenge myself.  Carving in Grey Linoleum proved successful the first time, with the 'Willow Tree'.  It started as one idea and turned into something completely different.  There has always been a third phase to the Grey Linoleum Pieces.  Hopefully that phase will come to fruition this year, 2026.  I would elude to it, but would not wish to detract from the current state of this artwork.

What follows is a second attempt at the Grey Linoleum Medium.  Something I had never imagined to find myself working in.  It has been a constant learning curve.  Compensating for texture, detail, depth, and lighting.  What proved to be most time consuming, was the determination on how the background blended with the mid-ground and foreground, how the snow flowed across the grounds, how snow pile upon the stone wall, how the wall's perspective slowly dwindles off into the distance.  the largest obstacle was the variance between the barn and the barren tree in front of it.  What I did not compensate for was the depth of the linoleum itself.  It did not provide enough to allow for a greater amount of depth, hence why it appears flat in some areas.  Like I say, learning curves.  Regardless, I enjoyed the variations and hey it looks awesome on a wall.

Material:  Grey Linoleum on Particle Board

Dimensions:  18"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.25"(d)

portfolio.title: A Little Shimmer
portfolio.description:
Bob Ross is one of many artists who have influence my painting style over the years.  I always found it fascinating at how quickly the man could produce a full painting in 25 minutes.  I have watched countless episodes of him walking through technique.  If you are not careful, he soothing voice will put you right to sleep on your favorite couch or easy chair.  It between his Happy Little Clouds and Trees, he introduced me to the "Wet on Wet" Technique.

I found myself compelled to try his repeated "Wet on Wet" Technique mostly due to what seemed like his effortless attempts at painting clouds and cloud cover.  It was absolutely mesmerizing.  I fore one struggled with painting clouds, and found the process painstakingly difficult, so what did I have to lose.

This piece represents my first of many attempts at Bob Ross' "Wet on Wet" Technique.  It is a continued practice of capturing everything beautiful about nature and the world about us.  

It was envisioned as a  fisherman's dream.  After a long day, the sun finally setting upon the horizon, the days work is tallied, and the distant dinner bell rings off in the distance.  Can you hear it?  The fisherman pulls in his lines and sets the bow towards the shoreline.  As the water laps the boat's sides, he reminisces at how fruitful his life has become.  

Material:  Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:  18"(w) x 36" (h) x 0.75"(d)

portfolio.title: In The Moment
portfolio.description:
When I started this piece, I knew it was meant for me and me alone.  It would not be for sale nor would it hang on another's wall.  At least in my lifetime.  This was a piece meant to challenge me, meant to force me to think outside of the box, as I applied different painting styles and contrasting colors.

So yes, it was meant to deliberately force myself out of my comfort zone.  How frustrating was it you ask?  Well I am confident that I had countless conversations questioning the 'Whys' and 'Hows'.

Different techniques were chosen for cloud cover, background, mid-ground, and foreground.  In addition, both contrasting colors and mid-ground were chosen as areas of focus.

The lesson I took away is that we must continuously strive to be a better person than the moment before.  By forcing myself to walk paths unknown are meant to sharpen the mind, to hone the skills necessary so that I can take on the next challenge, and the one after that.

Also it is worth noting, this is the first piece I have painted someone into the scenery.  That someone is me.  Seated upon the bench, admiring the landscape, and what had been created.  Of all of the paintings that I have done, it was my brother who remarked upon this particular piece on more than one occasion.  I had never let anyone know that it was I seated upon the bench, yet my brother knew immediately.  He recognized the back of my head by the huge swirling cowlick in my hairline.  This made me smile, because I spent countless hours looking at the back of my head, so it would look like me seated upon that very bench.  For my brother to have remarked on it though will always mean the world to me.  I think of him often

Material: Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with white gallery style edges

Dimensions:  24"(w) x 18" (h) x 0.75"(d)

portfolio.title: Graue Mill
portfolio.medium: Pencil and Ink on Matte board
portfolio.description:
Ahhh, this piece actually represents a rather large shift in my life.  I took on this piece about the time I went back to the University for a second Bachelors of Science Degree in Computer Science.  Mechanical Engineering just wasn't the field I thought it would be.  

With change also comes challenge.  Going back to the University, at Indiana State University, there in Terre Haute, Indiana, was probably the largest challenge I had taken on in my life, mostly because it is a journey I took on myself, of my own volition and accord.  

This piece was a different kind of challenge.  One that not only turned into an interesting piece, but allowed me to carry some of the techniques and lessons into many pieces to come.  This was the combination of a request by my mother, in addition to an attempt at "Pointillism".  It also represented my first attempt at using Inks with pencil work on top of Matte board.  

This, I am afraid, was a first and last attempt at "Pointillism".  I dreamed about this pieces for months afterwards.  Something about patiently and meticulously placing one pencil mark on a Matte board, over, and over, and over eventually finds its way into your subconscious mind.

With so many people desiring the original, in addition to my mother, I thought what better way to share the work with everyone, than through the distribution of prints.  Being its own learning curve, for I had never distributed prints.  I purchased my first flat-bed scanner and went for it.  

The piece now belongs to my first cousin on my father's side of the family.  As the years have progressed and our immediate family has diminished to a certain extent.  I rarely see her anymore, but wanted to make certain she had something from me, from that side of the family, if only to give her a story and allow her to reminisce. 

Material:  Pencil, Ink on Matte-board

Dimensions:  20"(w) x 16" (h) x 0.25"(d)

portfolio.title: Backyard #1
portfolio.description:
I was still living in the mid-west at the time.  It was probably one of the smallest apartments I had ever lived it.  Zionsville, Indiana, home of the Bentley if I do recall.  The apartment complex was nestled against a State funded Preserve.  It was one early morning, the snow had just covered the grounds about.  As I slowly awakened from my slumber, stumbled into the living room, I had noticed the wonderous beauty Mother Nature had bestowed upon us.  The view was through my living room sliding glass door but what I had envisioned was a portal into the Winter Wonder Land about.

This was a quick piece, one that I immensely enjoyed.  It was my first winter in that location.  Such a peaceful area.  Having only lived in Zionsville for a couple of years, I still reminisce in how beautiful that moment truly was.  

Long term the idea was to turn it into what appeared as a Window Frame looking out into the world, and then add a base with a coat rack.  Never got around to the coat rack.  There's still time.

portfolio.title: 1992 Playboy Playmate Portrait
portfolio.description:
A continued conquest to conquer the elusive portrait.  To draw is one thing, to draw portraits is quite another.  

One ear is lower than the other.  One eye might be higher than the other.  Pupils might not be the same exact size.  The Human Condition comes in all shapes and sizes.  There is no manner of perfection to be had.  

To hone that skill, to draw a portrait can be quite time consuming.   I had made several attempts prior to this one.  Where else was a young man to get beautiful models.  Ones that could be drawn over and over again until perfection, at least the perspective of perfection, was achieved.

The 1992 Playboy Playmate of the Year was probably one of my better attempts at that time in my life.  I do not recall her name, but I do recall that she road Harley Davidson's.  How cool I thought that was and could not wait to ride one of my own some day.

portfolio.title: Ones Perspective
portfolio.medium: Mixed Medium - Pencil, Pastel, Ink on Matte board
portfolio.description:
This started out as a vacation to Moab, Utah, the Mountain Biking Mecca of the nation.  An area that I have never been too, but was encouraged by a work colleague and friend, Paul Schumacher.  We had a lot of fun out west and I have returned many time since that first trip, so many years ago.  I always wondered what happened to ole Paul.

We trekked west to Moab, Utah to do some Mountain Biking, one of Paul's passions.  I went to try my hand at it, in addition to just hanging out at the National Parks and snapping some photography.  Back then I was still using a Canon EOS Rebel model, but it was a 32mm wind.  I miss that camera, something about taking pictures and winding to the next shot, was simply therapeutic.  Anyway, I was shooting some Black and Whites, while Paul was out biking the "Slick Rock" Course.

I enjoyed this particular photo so much that I started a pencil drawing of it shortly thereafter. Having only finished half of it, I hence put it away for a later day. There it sat for a decade until 2023. Having it sit for as long as it had, it gave me a new perspective on how to finish it. So I decided to dust it off and do just that.

Material:  Pencil, Pastel, Ink on Matte-board

Dimensions:  20"(w) x 16" (h) x 0.25"(d)

portfolio.title: LionFish
portfolio.description:
My goodness.  This piece actually started as a conversation at work.  I had moved to Florida back in 2017 and eventually found myself working at Allstate in Jacksonville, Florida.  There I met a fellow colleague who eventually became a fast friend, Dean Fudge.  

At the time we speaking about hobbies and what we enjoyed doing for fun.  He mentioned his many great adventures of scuba diving; whereas I spoke of my artwork.  He asked if I could paint him something, but it was paint on canvas he was seeking.  He wanted me to paint one of his aluminum of heavy metal diving tanks.  Something I had never done nor knew how to proceed.  I hesitated, suggesting that I had never done anything like that and would have to research it and get back with him.  As to the subject matter he wanted a shark or whale theme, possibly a varied array of fish and ocean theme.  What about the Lionfish I asked?  Eventually I found myself practicing and painted this piece.  Unfortunately it never found it's way to a diving tank, COVID shut down the industry and Dean Fudge eventually moved back to Canada.

Simply entitled Lion-fish, and they say Charlie Tuna, is the King of the Seas.  This is a colorful representation of a Lion-fish, lackadaisically swimming beneath the waters, amongst the beautiful coral reef.  This actually represents the first animal I have ever painted.  

Material: Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:  18"(w) x 24" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: The Path
portfolio.description:
This is the back porch view from my first apartment in Orlando, Florida.  It was a small little apartment, but it was next to work and a beautiful body of water.  

Beneath cool afternoon autumn skies, somewhere on the east side of Orlando, Florida a reflective body of water awaits.  Walking down to the shore-line you can hear the patter of geckos, the chirp of frogs, and the ever-so silent alligator skimming along the surface.  

A slender woman, whose attire is both relaxing and comforting.  A t-shirt, cutoff blue-jean shorts, and a large patterned checkered flannel flaps about her hips.  She walks her little dog and simply stops.  Taking the leash off the dog, she lets it go about it's business, as she finds herself seated along the path.  The mirrored surface reflecting every nuance of the cloud cover.  The periodic breeze blowing her flowing blond hair.  She breaths a sigh of relief and lets her shoulders relax.  She has found her moment on the path and it is blissful.

Material:  Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges 

Dimensions:  48"(w) x 24" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Ombrello Rosa
portfolio.description:
The story behind this piece is interesting. This originally started at as an Acrylic Painting. While in Zionsville, Indiana, I attended a "Wine n Canvas" some know it as a "Paint n Sip". It was not my first time attending and to be honest most would approach me and tell me I was cheating or I did not belong in such classes, because of the quality of work. I would go anyway, because they were fun for me and helped me practice my paint times.
​
Needless to say, this piece was done at the height of what I referred to as the "Lonely Umbrella" Movement. Everywhere you looked there was a painting of a woman with an umbrella, alone, walking the path of life.

When the evening's class had come to a close, my initial was to take it home and touch it up in a few areas. Well just touching up turned into a full over-paint with Oils. My first over-paint :)

Needless to say this was a fun piece to paint and now hangs on a friend's wall.

Material:  Acrylic & Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:  16"(w) x 20" (h) x 0.75"(d)

portfolio.title: Fathers Farm
portfolio.description:
I started this piece with a particular idea in mind.  It started its journey as a Commissioned Piece, one that I had promised an old high school friend of mine. 

As I continued to paint, I found myself beautifully enamored within the detail. The more detail I added the more my mind floated into bliss. I found myself painting trees, pasture, and grass without realizing what I was doing.  After a few hours it felt as if no sooner had I started, the sun had set across the horizon.  Day after day continued in this fashion.  I eventually found myself wondering why my feet hurt, turning to the clock, realizing that  5 sometimes 6 hours straight had lapsed. It is a beautiful world to find yourself engulfed by.  I love imagination.

Once completed, what started out as a commissioned piece, turned into a landscape similar to my grandfather, William Webster out in Elbridge Township, Illinois.  The barn was not quite identical, but the landscape, up and off to the right, as the field extends into the distance is very similar that I found myself walking to many years ago.  

As for my friend I promised her another, you may find it entitled 'Daddy's Barn'.  Once you compare the two you will see why I started anew.

Material:  Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with white gallery style edges

Dimensions:  24"(w) x 18" (h) x 0.75"(d)

portfolio.title: Maroon Bells
portfolio.medium: Mixed Medium - Pencil, Pastel, Ink on Matte board
portfolio.description:
The story behind this piece was quite a learning experience and to be honest, I have only used this technique one time.

During my young adult life I was creating the majority of my work on matte-board.  At one point, I was inspired to work with fine brushes and inks. Penciling in the landscape, I proceeded to apply a first coat of white ink.  Letting it dry.  The following session I proceeded to apply the black ink on-top of the white layer.  The end result was such a deflating moment.  Upon drying, the black ink peeled away from the white.  I truly was frustrated because it was something I did not expect.  So I paced the room, thinking of a way to apply multiple layers of ink, without having it peel away from the surface.

During this time, of thought, I decided to finish out the sky with pastel and pencil.

The solution eventually presented itself.  When I was a student at Southern Illinois University at Carbondale, I at one time hung out with an Architect Student, Greg Swerbinski (I am confident I am butchering his last name, so I apologize Greg).

I recalled that he used an aerosol product called "Workable Fix-it", for his classroom assignment sketches.  If it worked for his assignments, why not my project?

So I went to my favorite art store, "Hobby Lobby", and purchased a couple of cans.  With caution and patience, I applied a thin layer on-top of the white ink layer, let it dry, and proceeded to apply the black layer of ink. 

Imagine my excitement, when it worked, the black layer did not peel away, and i could proceed with the remaining layers of light tan, dark tan, and white highlights, only to follow with penciled shadowing.  The trick was applying a thin layer of "Workable Fix-it" on-top of each new layer of color.  Looking back I'm surprised I did not get nauseous from the fumes.  Needless, to say, I was able to work the project to completion.

Like i say this was a once in a life-time learning experience; hence why I have never attempted it again.  It just occurred to me that this was the last piece of artwork on matte-board.  I eventually moved back to paper and transitioned to canvas.

Material:  Ink, Pastel, Pencil on Matte-board

Dimensions:  33"(w) x 28" (h) x 0.25"(d)

portfolio.title: Doradus Nebula
portfolio.description:
This is a piece that I finished a very long time ago, early 2005ish if I recall.  It represents a time in my life where I felt lost, angry at the world, uncertain of what my path was or how I should even find it. Maybe that is simply the plight of a young man. In taking on this project, I wanted to test the level of detail I could achieve with each brush stroke.  Needless to say I wore out the tips of several. 

I consider this a Contemporary Piece because I have painted it across 2 separate 16x20" Museum Quality Canvases.  I also painted the edges of the canvases as if the nebula had engulfed them.  To post it here, I have merely stitched them together.

Some key words that might describe that point in my life; chaos, nebulous, space, twists, turns, unknown, uncertainty, slightly askew even

Material:  Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with the sides painted

Dimensions:  2 by (16"(w) x 20" (h) x 0.5"(d))

portfolio.title: Julie Hahn Portrait
portfolio.medium: Pencil, Pastel, Ink on Matte board
portfolio.description:
This is one of many favorites.  it was a commissioned piece that turn out so well I created a second for my own portfolio.  It was a year after I had graduated from Highschool.  My senior year, I participated in the Year Book Club.  There we worked as a team to write, create copy, create artwork, take photos only to assemble them and ship the prototype off to publishing and eventually distribution for that year.  I had a lot of fun in the Yearbook Club and did create several pieces of artwork, including the cover for the year 1989.  

Having such a large distribution, my name eventually got out to several as an artist.  So much so, that a very young gentleman, at the time, Jamie Littleton, approached me, requesting a commissioned piece of his girlfriend.  AKA, Julie Hahn.

I did not know Julie at the time.  To be honest, I never really knew her, nor were we in the same circle or groups.  Jamie did not have a good picture of her, yet insisted that I draw her portrait as a Birthday Gift.  He eventually convinced me to take on the work, so I shook his hand, and suggested that I would see what I could do.

I took it upon myself to knock on her families door, recanting the story, as to the request, and lack of photograph.  Her mother was extremely kind.  Insisted I come in, sit down, and offer me something to drink.  As we sat there, she found the idea delightful and was able to provide a Junior Year Highschool Yearbook Photo that I could work from.

This particular piece was fun.  It helped me to continue to break ground in mixing Pencil, Pastels, Inks all on Matte board.

portfolio.title: Avenue In The Rain
portfolio.description:
I first saw this piece on West Wing.  Anyone familiar with the television show would know that it was primarily based upon the nation's capital.  Ever since that time I have always been intrigued by the piece.  Its patriotic nature, it's sense of honor and duty.  For some reason it simply spoke to me.  

The history behind the painting is as follows...

"The Avenue in the Rain is a 1917 oil painting by the American Impressionist painter Childe Hassam. It depicts Fifth Avenue in New York City in the rain, draped with U.S. flags. The painting is one of six works by Hassam in the permanent art collection of the White House in Washington, D.C.

Between 1916 and 1919, Hassam completed as many as thirty paintings showing city streets decorated with flags. Hassam called this series "the flag series." The Avenue in the Rain dates to February 1917, a time when patriotic fervor was rising and about two months before the United States entered the First World War. The previous month, Germany had extended its unrestricted submarine warfare to neutral ships, including American vessels. The Zimmermann Telegram became public knowledge at the end of February 1917, and the country declared war on Germany on April 6, 1917.

The painting measures 42 by 22.25 inches (106.7 cm × 56.5 cm) and is dominated by tones of blue and red. A large U.S. flag is depicted in the foreground, while a number of smaller flags fill the surrounding space and line Fifth Avenue into the background. Several dark figures in the middle distance hold umbrellas. The flags seem to float in mid-air, hanging from poles that project from unseen buildings. Their images are reflected on the wet street and sidewalk. In painting this work and others in the series, Hassam may have been influenced by two similar pieces by Claude Monet depicting national celebrations in Paris on June 30, 1878.

The painting was donated to the White House in 1963 by Thomas Mellon Evans and hung between the windows in John F. Kennedy's blue-themed President's Bedroom (now a private sitting room, adjacent to the Yellow Oval Room on the second floor). It was in the President's Dining Room for many years and hung in the Oval Office during Bill Clinton's, Barack Obama's, and Donald Trump’s terms, as well as currently under Joe Biden's term."

As you can tell I have taken a few liberties with the piece, if only to make it my own.  I truly enjoyed painting this piece.  I took my time, relaxed, and created my own little world so to speak.  It is always fun and puts a smile upon my face.  Hopefully I have done Mr. Hassam justice.  One of these days I might add the spectators.  I have not decided yet.

Material:  Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:  24"(w) x 30" (h) x 0.75"(d)

portfolio.title: Winter Oasis
portfolio.medium: Acrylic Overpaint on Gray Textured Chiseled Linoleum
portfolio.description:
It seems I am always on a pursuit to challenge myself.  Carving in Grey Linoleum proved successful the first time, with the 'Willow Tree'.  It started as one idea and turned into something completely different.  There has always been three phases to the Grey Linoleum Pieces.  Hopefully that phase will come to fruition this year, 2026.  I would elude to it, but would not wish to detract from the current state of this artwork.

What follows is a second attempt at the Grey Linoleum Medium.  Something I had never imagined to find myself working in.  It has been a constant learning curve.  Compensating for texture, detail, depth, and lighting.  What proved to be most time consuming, was the determination on how the background blended with the mid-ground and foreground, how the snow flowed across the grounds, how snow pile upon the stone wall, how the wall's perspective slowly dwindles off into the distance.  the largest obstacle was the variance between the barn and the barren tree in front of it.  What I did not compensate for was the depth of the linoleum itself.  It did not provide enough to allow for a greater amount of depth, hence why it appears flat in some areas.  Like I say, learning curves.  

After all was said and done, I stood back and wondered what i was missing.  Was I missing something?   As I peered into the carved wonder it occurred to me, why not paint it now and so I did.

The sculpture was cleaned only to follow layer after layer, of oil paint.  The process was continued until the scene was saturated with a rich wintry color scheme to compliment it's depth

Material:  Grey Linoleum on Particle Board

Dimensions:  18"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.25"(d)

portfolio.title: Backyard #2
portfolio.description:
This Autumn Scene was a Commissioned Request made by my older cousins, on my mother's side of the family.  It was an idea and wish to be a Christmas Gift for their parents.  As we were having the conversation, I recall asking 'Are you sure?'  The question came shortly after the picture the presented for me to paint.   'Oh, yes, yes, that view, of the yard, is my father's favorite part of the house.  He always enjoys mowing that part of the yard.  He also enjoys the Autumn Colors each and every year.  They referred to it as their parent's inner serenity.

So I set out to paint the Autumn scene.  Starting in the background, slowly working my way to the foreground.  I was deliberately meticulous not only about the Autumn Colors, but the placement of them.  To paint someone's idea of serenity, was a little daunting to say the least.  I not only wanted it to pop, but I wanted it to portray and have the same manner of feelings as if they were truly in their own backyard.

It has been hanging on their wall since 2016.  My hope is they have enjoyed it as much as I have enjoyed painting it.

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with white gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
24"(w) x 18" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Daddys Barn
portfolio.description:
I have actually spoken to this piece in another artwork posting, 'Fathers Farm'.

This represents an second attempt at a Commissioned Piece.  One that an old high-school friend approached me on, years ago.  Her desire was a painting of one of her favorite childhood memories; her fathers old barn.  As you can imagine it took a little longer than originally anticipated as this was a second attempt.  To force me into an accurate representation and perspective I drew the image onto the canvas with a soft pencil prior to painting.  Rarely do I take this approach, only because I prefer the free flowing aspect of applying paint to canvas allowing my brain to find and determine the overall scope and breadth of such.

Needless so say, I eventually completed the commissioned piece, and happily delivered it to her, at the old Paris, Illinois McDonalds, prior to Christmas of 2019.

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:     
24"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Time
portfolio.description:
Over the course of our planet's fragile state, wind, water, and time have carved, molded and formed something ever so breathtaking. Serenity can be found in natural beauty. What better way to embrace it than to capture it's its meticulous detail onto canvas.

The original intent of this piece was to push myself, to add as much detail as possible.  Most was brushed with a 2.5" fine brush.  Sometimes I am surprised I did not wear it down to the knub.  

To this day I still recall painting this piece, in that small Orlando Apartment, just outside of downtown.  Looking out the window, through my enclosed lanai, onto the large body of reflective water, that would become its own painting.  

This piece was entered into a 2019 1340 Art Magazine, "Call For Artist". It made it to the final round however was not chosen for publication. I have found over the years what wins, tends to be considered ground braking, mixed mediums, contemporary, or outright abstract.

I will continue to push myself.  It would be nice to win a contest sometime within my lifetime.  I would take great pride in that.  That much is certain.

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Wrap Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
30"(w) x 30" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Montagna Numero 1
portfolio.description:
Another Bob Ross inspiration that proved to be a labor of love, with intermittent cussing of course.

Bob Ross's palette knife work always fascinated me.  His level of expertise and ease of application, in addition to mixing colors and mediums in real-time was uncanny and magical.

Having never attempted such a technique, I decided to throw myself into it.  What did I have to lose I thought.  It would be another learning experience and if I enjoyed it, I would then have added another technique to my toolkit.

This beautiful snow covered mountain scene is actually Moraine Lake, in Banff National Park, Alberta Canada.  Someday I will make my way to this profound beauty.  I have seen multiple images where the water is so clear, you can see the bottom of the lake.  Like so may pieces this particular beauty currently hangs amongst so many others.  Every-time I look at this piece I want to grab a rowboat and oars and just row to the center and dip my toes in the mirrored waters

Someday soon.

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
30"(w) x 24" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Mountain Face
portfolio.description:
The third attempt and final attempt in this series. 

It truly has been a journey and quite an experience. This was a new medium for me.  One I can definitely see exploring further. Something I had never imagined finding myself working in.  Other ideas have already come to mind so much so that as of late last year, 2025, I found myself working in Resin, Molding & Casting.  I have also had ideas for new Grey Linoleum Projects and found myself researching alternative distributors for larger sheets of linoleum inclusive of depth.  I would like to have thicker sheets so more definition is possible.  Things to come obviously.

With respect to this third attempt, compensating for texture, detail, depth, and lighting my original inspiration was Half Dome out in the state of California. It turned into a VanGogh'esk Impressionistic, Textured Scene.  To have never seen Half Dome until late 2025, I felt this turned out extremely well.  As I was making my way around Yosemite, I pulled over and walked my way to this face of Half Dome.  At first I was merely enamored with the unbelievable sense of natural beauty, then my eyes shifted directly in front of me where a couple was having their picture taken.  I briefly stood and enjoyed the moment, looking up as to their surroundings and what their picture would capture, was this face of 'Half Dome'.  I remember smiling without hesitation pointing and saying, "Hey, I recognize that".  Clearly I got some weird looks, but who cares.  I thought it was funny and clearly still do, because I am sharing it.  It was nice seeing my inspiration first-hand in real life.  

Yosemite is simply breathtaking and I could see traveling out there again.

I have already started painting the piece, however still appreciate its raw form.

Material:
Grey Linoleum on Particle Board

Dimensions:
18"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.25"(d)

portfolio.title: A Long Day
portfolio.description:
Bob Ross is one of many artists who have influence my painting style over the years.  I always found it fascinating at how quickly the man could produce a full painting in 25 minutes.  I have watched countless episodes of him walking through technique.  If you are not careful, he soothing voice will put you right to sleep on your favorite couch or easy chair.  It between his Happy Little Clouds and Trees, he introduced me to the "Wet on Wet" Technique.

I found myself compelled to try his repeated "Wet on Wet" Technique mostly due to what seemed like his effortless attempts at painting clouds and cloud cover.  It was absolutely mesmerizing.  I fore one struggled with painting clouds, and found the process painstakingly difficult, so what did I have to lose.

This piece represents my first of many attempts at Bob Ross' "Wet on Wet" Technique.  It is a continued practice of capturing everything beautiful about nature and the world about us.  

Entitled A Long Day -- Some days seem like they last for an eternity; whereas others pass with the blink of an eye.  My preference, of course, being the former. This was a wonderful piece to paint.  One of my favorite compliments was from my father.  'How in the world did he paint all of those trees?  Look at how tight they are.'  It still makes me smile thinking of the moment.  

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
30"(w) x 24" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Willow Tree
portfolio.description:
It seems I am always on a pursuit to challenge myself.  Carving in Grey Linoleum proved successful.  It started as one idea and turned into something completely different.  There has always been three phases to the Grey Linoleum Pieces.  Hopefully that phase will come to fruition this year, 2026.  I would elude to it, but would not wish to detract from the current state of this artwork.

A first attempt at this new medium.  My initial premise was to diligently carve the willow tree, then use it as a traditional print block to mass produce prints.  Yet the more I worked on it, the more I focused on texture and depth, the more I realized it would eventually find itself framed and hanging on a wall. 

The idea set me on a path to eventually to create more, with continued emphasis on light, shadow, texture, and detail. 

Material: 
Grey Linoleum on Particle Board

Dimensions:      
18"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.25"(d)

portfolio.title: Kimberly Conrad Portrait
portfolio.description: What better way to perfect my drawings skills by sketching Hugh Heffner's Wife.  Well one of many wives.  This particular one makes me smile, because it took countless erasures to finally produce a portrait I was proud of.  The piece also mimics another artist's technique, one that he was very well known for, Patrick Nagel.  It is interesting, as a young man I can remember how great I thought Playboy was.  Not only did it have everything a young man desired, but the artwork was phenomenal, the playmates were my models, and it even had the occasional article that was worth a read.  Nagel proved to be one of my influences over the course of my life, as an artist.

portfolio.title: Painted Face
portfolio.medium: Acrylic Overpaint on Gray Textured Chiseled Linoleum
portfolio.description:
The third attempt and final attempt in this series. 

It truly has been a journey and quite an experience. This was a new medium for me.  One I can definitely see exploring further. Something I had never imagined finding myself working in.  Other ideas have already come to mind so much so that as of late last year, 2025, I found myself working in Resin, Molding & Casting.  I have also had ideas for new Grey Linoleum Projects and found myself researching alternative distributors for larger sheets of linoleum inclusive of depth.  I would like to have thicker sheets so more definition is possible.  Things to come obviously.

With respect to this third attempt, compensating for texture, detail, depth, and lighting my original inspiration was Half Dome out in the state of California. It turned into a VanGogh'esk Impressionistic, Textured Scene.  To have never seen Half Dome until late 2025, I felt this turned out extremely well.  As I was making my way around Yosemite, I pulled over and walked my way to this face of Half Dome.  At first I was merely enamored with the unbelievable sense of natural beauty, then my eyes shifted directly in front of me where a couple was having their picture taken.  I briefly stood and enjoyed the moment, looking up as to their surroundings and what their picture would capture, was this face of 'Half Dome'.  I remember smiling without hesitation pointing and saying, "Hey, I recognize that".  Clearly I got some weird looks, but who cares.  I thought it was funny and clearly still do, because I am sharing it.  It was nice seeing my inspiration first-hand in real life.  

Yosemite is simply breathtaking and I could see traveling out there again.

Similar to the previous carved piece, layer upon layer upon layer of oil paint was applied.  I guess I never realized how linoleum soaks up oil paint.   Having seen Half Dome I would most likely have chosen different colors for the Rock Face.  The sky itself turned out like ripples in a stream, flowing carelessly across the horizon, as puffs of clouds float about the rock face.  The foreground was meant to be an intermixed of tall waist level grass, billowed by the flowing streams of air on high.  Overall l was pleased with the journey and its final piece.

I definitely see continuing in this medium, that is for certain.

Material:
Grey Linoleum on Particle Board

Dimensions:
18"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.25"(d)

portfolio.title: Ponte di Colori
portfolio.description:
If you have not been able to tell, I am originally from the mid-west.  I grew up with all 4 seasons.  The autumn months have always been my favorite.  It is the one season out of the year, where mother nature gets to be an artist too. 

A brief stroll through one of the many Indiana State Parks, on a cool Autumn afternoon. Only to come upon an old masonic wonder. One that has stood the test of time.  Layers upon layers of varied hues and mixed leaves, painted one on top of another, providing depth, providing a sense of the surreal, as if you can feel the cool Autumn Breeze upon your brow and smell the roasting hotdogs and caramelized marshmallows wafting on front of your nose.  Can you envision it.  

I know that was my inspiration. 

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
16"(w) x 20" (h) x 0.75"(d)

portfolio.title: Winters Lane
portfolio.description:
After years of wanting a traditional easel, I finally broke down and purchased an H-Frame Easel in 2021.  No sooner did I order it, that I waited with great anticipation as to its arrival.  The day it showed up at my apartment door was an exciting one.  The delivery man wasn't happy, but I know I was.

It took the better part of a day to assemble the Easel.  At first attempt I thought I was missing some pieces and hardware, but it was buried within the styrofoam within the encased cardboard box.   Successfully assembled, I immediately unwrapped the plastic from an empty white canvas and set it onto the Easel.  "What should my first painting be?", I asked in the silence of the hobby room.  There the empty canvas sat, week, after week, until finally I stepped in, through colors onto a canvas, and said, 'Enough of this, lets get this started and see what happens."  I love moments like this, true pure imagination took over.  It eventually proved to be my first major palette knife piece.  I let the shape of the palette knife determine the shape and depth of the landscape.

Hiking through the mountains to the other side. The hiker's senses are at ease yet heightened as they work their way through. Eventually a clearing presents itself. The sense of peace and confidence fills the soul as the lane presents the next opportunity. Does the hiker accept such a fate? We may never know, but the  moment is breathtaking.

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
36"(w) x 24" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: Wheatfield with Crows
portfolio.description:
I have always enjoyed the Impressionistic, Neo-impressionistic era, Van Gogh being my favorite, Monet, Manet, Seurat there are countless.  That era has always been a motivator, to push myself, to drive towards a better version of me.  A better version of me, who can in turn produce a better version of art.  Sounds slightly egotistical I realize, but it has helped me to grow and change as a human being.  One I feel is better, one that I believe is better, and better of for such growth.

One of my first attempts at oil painting was Starry Night by Van Gogh.  Followed by my second, San Giorgio Maggiore at Twilight, by Claude Monet, my third attempt at oil painting was Autumn as Nuenen, by Van Gogh.  All of these pieces were eventually given to family members so I never really captured descent pictures of them to add to my portfolio.  I have San Giorgio, posted here.  My hope is that I will eventually capture the other two for prosperity's sake.  There is a little story about 'Starry Night' that I enjoy sharing, but not here.  At least not until I get a good picture of it to post.

How I truly enjoy painting the masters.

Wheat field with Crows is another Van Gogh attempt.  One I feel turned out well.  Another I hope to attempt in the near future is Starry Night over the River Rhone.  That has always been another favorite.

Material: 
Oil on Museum Quality Canvas with black painted gallery style edges

Dimensions:      
24"(w) x 12" (h) x 1.5"(d)

portfolio.title: San Giorgio Maggiore At Twilight
portfolio.description:
I have always enjoyed the Impressionistic, Neo-impressionistic era, Van Gogh being my favorite, Monet, Manet, Seurat there are countless.  That era has always been a motivator, to push myself, to drive towards a better version of me.  A better version of me, who can in turn produce a better version of art.  Sounds slightly egotistical I realize, but it has helped me to grow and change as a human being.  One I feel is better, one that I believe is better, and better of for such growth.

One of my first attempts at oil painting was Starry Night by Van Gogh.  Followed by my second, San Giorgio Maggiore at Twilight, by Claude Monet, my third attempt at oil painting was Autumn as Nuenen, by Van Gogh.  All of these pieces were eventually given to family members so I never really captured descent pictures of them to add to my portfolio.  I have San Giorgio, posted here.  My hope is that I will eventually capture the other two for prosperity's sake.  There is a little story about 'Starry Night' that I enjoy sharing, but not here.  At least not until I get a good picture of it to post.

How I truly enjoy painting the masters.

Hopefully i will get a better picture some day, the camera light bounced off of the painting.  To see it hanging in my parents den is a moment of pride for me.  They have had countless friends and colleagues both comment and compliment the pieces that hang in their house.  One in particular suggested the enjoyed how the light and colors reflect off of the river's surface.

Material:  Acrylic & Oil on Museum Quality Canvas

Dimensions:  20"(w) x 16" (h) x 0.50"(d)

portfolio.title: Lavanda
portfolio.description:
The story behind this piece is interesting. This originally started at as an Acrylic Painting. While in Indiana, I attended several "Wine n Canvas" Events.  It was not my first time attending and to be honest most would approach me and tell me I was cheating or I did not belong in such classes, because of the quality of work. I would go anyway, because they were fun for me and helped me practice my paint times.  I also enjoyed the pure pleasure of others embracing the wonderful world of art.

A Tuscan Landscape, a gentle breeze, a faint smell of lavender in the air. It represents all the soothing aspects of the senses.  I enjoyed the scene so, that I eventually brought it home.  Placed it upon my easel and repainted so of the areas, adding additional color, depth, and definition.  

Material: 
Acrylic & Oil on Museum Quality Wrap Canvas with white gallery style edges

Dimensions:  20"(w) x 16" (h) x 0.50"(d)

portfolio.title: 420 at the Watertower
portfolio.description:
I started this project April of 2022. 

This is a well known Chicago Landmark, the Water Tower.  It has always been one of my favorite Chicago sites.   To walk up to it, to sit in front of it and enjoy its ornate beauty is timeless.  

A little history behind the Water Tower.

"Built in 1869 by architect William W. Boyington, the Chicago Water Tower was designed to house a 138-foot standpipe that regulated water pressure for the city’s new lake-intake system.  Constructed from yellow Joliet limestone in a Castellated Gothic Revival style, it is the second-oldest water tower in the United States, preceded only by the Louisville Water Tower. 

The tower gained iconic status as one of the few structures to survive the Great Chicago Fire of 1871, largely because firefighter Frank Trautman covered it in water-soaked woolen blankets to protect it from cinders.  Although it became functionally obsolete in 1906 and faced multiple demolition threats, public outcry preserved the landmark, which was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1971. 

Today, the 182.5-foot structure at 806 North Michigan Avenue no longer serves as a water facility; instead, it houses the City Gallery, an art gallery showcasing local photographers and filmmakers."

It is currently a that is a work in progress.  There will come a day, where I will eventually dust off the cobwebs and finish this nostalgic piece.  

Oil on museum quality canvas, 30 in x 30 in x 1.5 in.

portfolio.title: Oceanic Delight
portfolio.description:
I started this project July of 2021. I was making significant progress and then I hit the proverbial imaginative brick wall. The inspiration for this piece was the result of an exchange of ideas with a Jacksonville friend of mine.  

It is currently a that is a work in progress.  There will come a day, where I will eventually dust off the cobwebs and finish this watery wrestling match.  

Oil on museum quality canvas, 40 in x 30 in x 2 in.

portfolio.title: Johnson Family Photo
portfolio.description: The Family Portrait -- my father actually requested this piece.  He wanted something to hang in the den.  To this day, it continues to hang on the den walls.  What is interesting about this piece, is that we did not sit for this photograph nor does one exist.  This was a series of piecemealed images, starting with my father and working my way from left to right.  At one point i found myself using drivers licenses images to finish the overall likeness of each family member.  Mom's only request was to make her beautiful.  At the time of drawing her hair was not that long.  That was one liberty I took with mom's portrait.

portfolio.title: Michelle Lynn Schmidt Portrait
portfolio.description:
My last, albeit favorite portrait.  

There may come a day where I might do another.  As of this moment, this particular piece retains the 'Last' Title.

It was definitely a labor of love only because I was head over heals for the woman.  Like so many portraits before, I worked deliberately and meticulously at this particular piece.  It was to be considered a gift and by god must be perfect.  

I recall needing an enlarged image of the photograph to get a better understanding of the detail within the portrait itself.  The dark shirt prevented me from finding the necessary shadow and depth desired.  There were also areas of the photo slightly blurred or who knows maybe aged to the point definition was worn away.  To be honest, I do not recall now.  I only recall that I needed enlarged copies to proceed.  At least in my own mind.

At the time, I was in Carbondale, Illinois and no one would allow me to enlarge the existing photo due to copyright infringement.  At the time all photographers and developers places there company's logo and signature on the back of photos.  Determined to enlarge the photograph, I thought how to get around this little conundrum.  Then it occurred to me that my favorite Student Union Bookstore sold Ink Erasures.   So I trotted off to purchase a 0.50 cent Ink Erasure.  

Wouldn't you know it, the company and photographer name no longer existed on the back of the photo.  Two days later I picked up the enlarged copies and happily finished the portrait.  

Ahhhhhhhh, fond memories.

publications.title: A Fractured I.T.
publications.subtitle: A Cautionary Tale of Artificial Intelligence and the Human Cost of Innovation
publications.author: Maurice C. Johnson III
publications.series: Part of the Agentic AI & Productivity Wave Series
description: Artificial intelligence was supposed to transform work. It did - but not in the way many workers expected.
In A Fractured I.T., software engineer Maurice Johnson draws on more than 24 years in information technology to examine what happens when AI stops being a tool and becomes a mandate.

Part memoir, part industry critique, and part warning, A Fractured I.T. explores OpenAI's catalytic role in the current AI wave, the pressure automation places on engineers and job seekers, and the urgent need for ethics, guardrails, and human-in-the-loop systems.

For readers interested in AI, careers, technology, and the human cost of automation, this is a candid look at the future of work from the perspective of someone living it-and it asks the question too many companies avoid: what happens when efficiency becomes more valuable than people?
Grab your copy today and join the conversation about our collective digital future.

reviews.quote:
The book does a great job of explaining how agentic AI is reshaping the way IT functions, especially in environments where systems, teams, and tools are already fragmented. Instead of glossing over complexity, it leans into it. The author breaks down how automation is evolving from simple task execution into something closer to autonomous decision-making, and what that means for productivity, governance, and day-to-day operations.

What stood out most to me was the practical lens. There are plenty of conceptual ideas here, but they're consistently tied back to real-world implications-how teams might actually implement these systems, where things can go wrong, and why "fractured" IT environments are both a challenge and an opportunity.

Overall, this book feels timely. It doesn't overhype AI, but it also doesn't underestimate how disruptive this shift could be. If you're trying to understand where AI-driven productivity is actually headed-and what it might look like inside imperfect, real organizations-this is worth your time.
reviews.author: Lindsey Davis

reviews.quote: This book is a compelling look at the impact of AI on today's workforce and corporate culture. The book combines real-world experience with thoughtful insight, exploring both the opportunities and challenges created by rapid technological change. I think it's important because it helps when you connect it to real world experiences. You can understand the concept of AI better. The writing style makes complex topics easy to understand while encouraging readers to think critically about the future of work. Overall, it's an engaging and timely read for anyone interested in artificial intelligence, innovation, and the human side of technology.
reviews.author: Tavia Patterson

reviews.quote: Such a great book! I feel that it mirrors what is happening today, and it's an eye-opener too. Great story and information, very interesting, and highly recommended.
reviews.author: Cassandra

reviews.quote: A Fractured I.T. is a thoughtful and timely look at the human side of artificial intelligence and workplace automation. Maurice Johnson combines personal experience with sharp industry insight to explore how AI is reshaping hiring, careers, and the future of work. Honest, accessible, and deeply relevant, this book asks important questions about ethics, efficiency, and what gets lost when technology moves faster than humanity. A compelling read for anyone interested in AI, technology, or modern careers.
reviews.author: Robert

reviews.quote: This was a really interesting read. I liked how it mixed technology, AI, and business topics without making it feel overly technical or hard to understand. The book does a good job showing how modern I.T. and productivity are changing while also touching on the human side of adapting to new technology. It felt relevant to where the workplace and AI are heading today. Overall, it's a good read for anyone curious about digital transformation, and the future of work.
reviews.author: Daevanna

pressRelease.headline: A FRACTURED I.T. EXAMINES THE HUMAN COST OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE IN CORPORATE AMERICA
pressRelease.subhead: Mr Maurice C. Johnson III delivers a gripping and timely account of disruption, resilience, and the real world consequences of rapid AI adoption.
paragraphs: In his powerful new book A Fractured I.T., author Maurice C. Johnson III offers a deeply personal and eye opening exploration of how artificial intelligence is transforming the modern workforce. Drawing from over two decades of experience as a software engineer, Johnson presents a candid narrative that captures the uncertainty, disruption, and human toll behind today's accelerating wave of technological change.

Blending memoir with industry analysis, A Fractured I.T. provides readers with an unfiltered look into the inner workings of corporate decision making during a time of rapid AI integration. Johnson recounts his own experience of professional displacement after raising concerns about unchecked innovation, while also examining broader systemic issues such as automated hiring systems, algorithm driven management, and the growing disconnect between human workers and machine led processes. The book introduces critical concepts such as the so called productivity wave and questions whether it represents progress or peril.

The inspiration for the book stems from Johnson's firsthand encounter with the shifting dynamics of the technology sector. Faced with unexpected career upheaval, he was compelled to document not only his personal journey but also the wider implications for professionals across industries. His narrative reflects both frustration and insight, offering a grounded perspective on how technological enthusiasm can sometimes overshadow ethical responsibility and long term consequences.

This work speaks to a wide audience that extends far beyond the technology field. From recent graduates entering an increasingly automated job market to seasoned professionals grappling with career uncertainty, readers will find relevance in Johnson's reflections. The book also serves as a call to action for business leaders, policymakers, and innovators to consider the human dimension of technological advancement and to pursue more balanced and thoughtful approaches to integration.

Maurice C. Johnson III brings authority and authenticity to the subject through his 24 year career in software engineering. His voice is both analytical and deeply human, combining technical knowledge with lived experience. His ability to translate complex industry trends into relatable insights makes A Fractured I.T. a compelling and accessible read for anyone concerned about the future of work.

With urgency and clarity, A Fractured I.T. challenges readers to rethink the trajectory of artificial intelligence and its impact on society. It stands as both a cautionary tale and a message of resilience in an era defined by rapid change.

contactCallout.title: Review Copies, Interviews, and Additional Information
contactCallout.copy: For review copies, interview requests, or additional information, please contact Maurice C. Johnson III.

domains.title: AI UNIT Calculator
domains.description:
AI UNIT Calculator is a strategic cost-planning web application for comparing AI infrastructure paths: cloud GPU rental, hardware ownership, and public API consumption.

The application estimates total cost of ownership for AI workloads using configurable GPU, API model, pricing, utilization, token volume, project term, and report-tier assumptions. It is intended for scenario planning, executive review, and early-stage infrastructure strategy.

The calculator supports:
features: Cloud rental versus hardware ownership comparisons.
Public API versus self-hosted infrastructure roadmap reporting.
Admin-managed market data with guarded live refreshes.
Tiered paid PDF report export using PayPal.
domains.note: Pricing outputs and generated reports are planning estimates, not binding quotes.
